name: SMACS 0723
introduction: Every speck of light in this image that isn't a sharp six-pointed star is an entire galaxy — each one containing hundreds of billions of suns. You're looking at the deepest infrared image of the universe ever captured, a patch of sky no bigger than a grain of sand held at arm's length. The cluster at center, SMACS 0723, acts as a gravitational lens: its combined mass is so immense that it bends spacetime itself, warping and magnifying the light of galaxies far behind it. Those arc-shaped streaks curving around the cluster's core? Each is a single galaxy stretched into a crescent by gravity's optical trick. The most distant objects here emitted their light over 13 billion years ago — when the universe was less than 5% its current age. You are receiving ancient light that has been traveling since nearly the beginning of everything. Pan slowly and let the scale settle in. Each hotspot here pulls you into a different chapter of cosmic history.
